experimental_studies.paper_title: Mitosis domain generalization in histopathology images - The MIDOG challenge
experimental_studies.bias_type: Scanner/domain shift (covariate shift caused by different whole slide scanners); inter-rater variability in mitotic figure annotation; batch effects from staining procedures and optical parameters
experimental_studies.fairness_metric: Not Reported (NR) - Domain generalization performance measured via F1-score across different scanners

experimental_studies.performance: Best approach (AI medical team): F1 = 0.748 (95% CI: 0.704-0.781); Reference method: F1 = 0.718; CNN baseline: F1 = 0.698; Top5 ensemble: F1 = 0.773; Performance varied by scanner: Scanner A (in-domain): 0.620-0.848; Scanner D (unlabeled): 0.375-0.745; Scanner E (unseen): 0.582-0.808; Scanner F (unseen): 0.550-0.790
experimental_studies.key_findings: The MIDOG 2021 challenge evaluated domain generalization for mitotic figure detection across different whole slide scanners. The winning approach achieved F1=0.748, performing at expert level. Domain-adversarial training, stain normalization, GAN-based augmentation, and Fourier domain adaptation were effective mitigation strategies. Performance dropped significantly on unseen scanners (0.375-0.808 vs 0.620-0.848 in-domain), demonstrating the severity of scanner bias. Ensemble methods and domain adaptation techniques improved generalization to unseen scanners.
experimental_studies.verification_notes: Domain generalization is a fairness issue - scanner bias creates unequal performance across institutions. Multi-center challenge with real-world evaluation.
